entry.title: Automating SAP HANA Cloud (Other Environment) DB Lifecycle Management with SAP Automation Pilot
entry.content:
In our recent updates around AI-enabled Ops Operations, we demonstrated how SAP Automation Pilot boosts structured, scalable, and intelligent automation for Day-2 Ops Agents through Joule.

Today, I am excited to take this one step further.

🚀  We are expanding our automation coverage to fully address SAP HANA Cloud lifecycle management in the Other Environment - bringing operational excellence to database management at scale.

From Standard Catalogs to Broader Lifecycle Automation

SAP Automation Pilot already provides a built-in catalog for managing SAP HANA Cloud database instances running in the Cloud Foundry environment - that is the the well-known SAP BTP Database Lifecycle Management (dblm-sapcp) Catalog.

This catalog has successfully helped customers automate essential lifecycle tasks directly within SAP BTP.

However, many customers also operate SAP HANA Cloud instances in the Other Environment, where lifecycle management is equally critical and operationally sensitive. While SAP Automation Pilot already offers all the technical capabilities required to automate those tasks, we recognized the need to package common scenarios into a structured, reusable, and publicly available catalog.

That is exactly what we have now delivered! 

Introducing: SAP HANA Cloud Lifecycle Management Sample Catalog (Other Environment)

We have consolidated a wide range of SAP HANA Cloud lifecycle scenarios into a dedicated public sample catalog, now available in our GitHub repository here: SAP HANA Cloud Lifecycle Management Sample Catalog (Other Environment)

This catalog provides a comprehensive and validated set of automation commands for managing SAP HANA Cloud instances at subaccount level using Service Manager.

Instead of executing repetitive and sensitive database operations manually, you can now automate domains such as: 🔹 Instance Lifecycle Management 🔹Backup & Recovery 🔹 Snapshot Management 🔹 Instance Configuration🔹 Upgrades & Plugins 🔹 Elastic Compute Nodes (ECN), etc. 

✅ All commands are validated and ready to be explored by our users - to help you minimize manual effort, reduce risk, and standardize operational procedures.

Beyond Automation: Toward Intelligent Database Operations

While structured lifecycle automation already delivers immediate value, the real transformation begins when automation becomes agentic.

Because all HANA Cloud-related commands are exposed through SAP Automation Pilot, they can be orchestrated, combined, and consumed in higher-level operational workflows. 

This naturally leads to the next evolution - AI Ops Agents for HANA Cloud enabled with SAP Automation Pilot and Joule! 

📖Be Part of the Journey - Join Our Customer Engagement Initiative

As we continue expanding agentic AI operations, collaboration with customers remains key.

If you would like to gain hands-on experience with AI-enabled Ops automation and help shape the future of intelligent database operations, we invite you to subscribe in our ongoing Customer Engagement Initiative: Agentic AI Operations with SAP Automation Pilot

Through this initiative, you can:

  • Explore real-world AI-driven automation scenarios;
  • Bring your own use cases and operational challenges;
  • Provide direct feedback to the product team;
  • Influence upcoming capabilities;

⏳ Registration is open until March 15, 2026 - secure your spot soon!

From structured lifecycle automation to fully agentic database operations - this is the next chapter of operational excellence with SAP Automation Pilot.

Let’s build it together!

entry.title: Customize SAPUI5 apps for business users – make an impact by joining our CEI project
entry.content:
SAP Customer Engagement Initiatives (CEI) on https://influence.sap.com allow you, as an SAP customer or partner, to gain early insights into SAP's product developments and work directly with SAP teams to shape future product directions. This project presents a unique opportunity to work with SAPUI5 flexibility product managers and engineering teams, leveraging your expertise customizing SAPUI5 apps with key user adaptation.

Join the upcoming CEI project to make an impact on how key users interact with key user adaptation

If you’re already convinced – join here. If not, read on 🙂

What is key user adaptation?

Key user adaptation is a mode that allows extending SAPUI5 applications for other users right in the browser. It is highly intuitive and requires no technical or programming skills. Just use drag and drop to make UI changes – no modification or copy of SAP standard coding necessary! Read more here.

What is the targeted improvement?

Although the product is mature and widely used, we actively evolve it by delivering new functionality and capability enhancements alongside continuous user experience improvements, informed by UI testing and direct customer feedback. 

We are in the process of overhauling key user adaptation’s UI, simplifying the overall approach while making often-used scenarios more streamlined. We’ve taken a close look at the version history (of activated adaptations) and how our users interact with it, extended the “embed third-party content” feature, widened the scope of custom fields that can be added to the UI – and more.

To ensure that we are on the right way, we need your help!

Who should participate?

Users who want to adapt standard SAPUI5 apps for business users. Both key users and technical users are welcome.

Why should you participate?

  • Gain understanding of SAPUI5 flexibility
  • Chance to influence a central SAPUI5 feature
  • Early access to prototypes

How can you participate?

Don’t miss out on this opportunity to share your feedback and engage with SAPUI5 flexibility development teams. Register by March 15, 2026, and make a true impact on how you can customize SAP apps to your needs!

Click here to sign up for our project. (A short overview about how to get started on influence.sap.com can be found here).

After you register for a project, you will be invited to an introductory call where you will get more information. This call is followed by individual or grouped feedback sessions.

Thank you in advance for helping us shape the future of SAPUI5 flexibility!

entry.title: Day-to-day SAP Business Technology Platform operations as part of a clean core approach
entry.content:
We talk a lot about clean core.  But what happens after go-live?

Honestly, that’s where the story gets interesting.

In our webinar, “Day-to-day SAP Business Technology Platform operations as part of a clean core approach,” we’ll unpack how to confidently operate extensions on SAP Business Technology Platform, even in hybrid setups working side by side with SAP S/4HANA.

Think of it like maintaining a high-performance engine.  It’s not just about adding powerful components.  It’s about tuning, monitoring, and making sure everything runs in sync.

We’ll cover account structures, multi-tenancy strategies, delivery models, and observability.  Real-world considerations.  No theory overload.  Just what you need to keep customer landscapes stable and scalable.

If you support customers day to day, this session will resonate.  And maybe even make your next project smoother.

Join us. Let’s turn clean core into a lasting operational advantage.

Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/feb/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013041.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #CleanCoreStrategy #HybridCloud #SAPS4HANA #PartnerEnablement

entry.title: From Prompt to Production: Creating an MCP Server for Joule Studio Using AI Core
entry.content:
Introduction

Hi, I'm Max McPhee, a BTP Solution Advisor at SAP. There are a lot of new capabilities coming to the SAP Business Suite, but what excites me most is that we can now build components for AI agents entirely within BTP and host those components in BTP—making it the one-stop shop for enterprise agents.

In this blog post, I'll walk you through how I built a simple MCP (Model Context Protocol) server from scratch, using AI-powered development tools, and deployed it to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry for integration with Joule Studio. The best part? The entire development process was done using conversation development with GenAI tools from SAP. 

With "MCP" literally in my name, I was obligated to dive into building an MCP server. Now let's get started.

 

What is MCP?

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard originally invented by Anthropic that enables AI systems to interact with external tools and data sources in a standardized way. Think of it as a universal language that allows AI agents to discover and use tools without custom integration code for each one.

SAP has been an early supporter of MCP, recognizing its potential for enterprise AI scenarios. The company has released several open-source MCP servers to the community, including:

  • UI5 MCP Server - For SAPUI5 development assistance
  • CAP MCP Server - For SAP Cloud Application Programming Model
  • Fiori MCP Server - For SAP Fiori development

For more details on SAP's MCP, check out SAP Build Introduces New MCP Servers to Enable Agentic Development.

With Joule Studio, you can connect your own custom MCP servers to extend Joule's capabilities with tools specific to your business needs.

 

Why would you want to do this?

Creating your own MCP server lets you extend Joule Studio with custom capabilities tailored to your business processes. You can connect to SAP systems, internal APIs, or any data source to give Joule new skills that understand your specific business context.

Requirements

  • SAP BTP Cloud Foundry account
  • Node.js installed locally
  • Basic understanding of JavaScript/Node.js
  • Access to Joule Studio with a SAP Build developer license

 

The Development Journey: AI All the Way Down

Here's where it gets meta. I used AI tools to build a tool for AI. Let me break down the process:

Step 1: Planning with Joule for Consultants

I started by asking Joule for Consultants to help me create a development plan for building an MCP server that could be deployed to SAP BTP. Joule helped me understand the architecture requirements, the SDK I'd need, and the configuration for Joule Studio integration.

 

Step 2: Building with an AI-Powered IDE

I took the plan from Joule and used it as the starting prompt in Cline, an AI coding assistant powered by models from SAP AI Core. The AI helped me:

  • Set up the Node.js project with the correct dependencies
  • Navigate the MCP SDK's API
  • Configure the streamable HTTP transport that Joule Studio requires
  • Discover the schemas necessary for proper argument handling

Step 3: Iterative Development

The AI didn't get everything right on the first try and that's okay! This is exactly why we are starting with a proof of concept scope. Through iterative debugging, we discovered important details like:

  • The MCP SDK requires a different schema than the plan expected
  • Session management is handled automatically via the mcp-session-id header
  • The transport must be "streamable HTTP" for Joule Studio compatibility

This is the beauty of AI-assisted development: rapid iteration with an intelligent partner. If I was doing this manually I would do the same thing, I would test what I built and address the errors I get in the logs, but with a coding assistant that work is done for me and I can focus on the outcome.

 

The Architecture

The end-to-end flow looks like this:

Joule for Consultants → AI-Powered IDE → MCP Server → BTP Cloud Foundry → Joule Studio

Key Components:

  • Runtime: BTP Cloud Foundry
  • MCP SDK: @modelcontextprotocol/sdk
  • Transport: Streamable HTTP (required for Joule Studio)
  • BTP Services: Cloud Foundry runtime, Destinations service

 

Building the MCP Server

Let me share the key pieces of the implementation.

Note: The code snippets below are excerpts to illustrate the key patterns. This blog is intended to share my experience building an MCP server on BTP, not serve as a step-by-step implementation guide.

Project Dependencies

{
  "name": "mcp-echo-server",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "type": "module",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "node server.js"
  },
  "engines": {
    "node": ">=18.0.0"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk": "^1.26.0",
    "express": "^5.2.1"
  }
}

 

The Echo Tool

Here's the core pattern for registering a tool with the MCP SDK:

import { McpServer } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/mcp.js";
import { z } from "zod";

// Define the input schema using Zod
const echoInputSchema = z.object({
  message: z.string().describe("Input message to process")
});

// Register the tool
server.tool(
  "echo",
  "Returns 'bar' when input is 'foo'. Use this to test MCP connectivity.",
  echoInputSchema.shape,
  async (args, extra) => {
    const message = args.message;

    if (message === "foo") {
      return {
        content: [{ type: "text", text: "bar" }]
      };
    }

    return {
      content: [{ type: "text", text: `Echo: ${message}` }]
    };
  }
);

Key Learning: The MCP SDK requires Zod schemas for proper argument handling. Plain JSON Schema objects won't work—a detail we discovered through debugging!

 

Cloud Foundry Manifest

---
applications:
  - name: mcp-echo-server
    memory: 512M
    disk_quota: 1G
    instances: 1
    buildpacks:
      - nodejs_buildpack
    random-route: true
    env:
      NODE_ENV: production
    health-check-type: http
    health-check-http-endpoint: /health

I've set memory to 512MB which is larger than needed for this simple echo server, but I'm using this as a MVP that I plan to expand. Starting with a reasonable production baseline avoids having to reconfigure everything later.

 

Deploying to SAP BTP

Deploying via BTP Cockpit

While you can use CF CLI I chose to do the deployment manually with the BTP Cockpit:

  1. Navigate to Cloud Foundry → Spaces → Your Space
  2. Click Deploy Application
  3. Upload your ZIP file
  4. Select manifest.yml for configuration
  5. Deploy and wait for the application to start

 

Configuring the Destination

This is critical for Joule Studio integration. Create an HTTP destination with these settings:

Property Value

Namemcp-echo-server
TypeHTTP
URLhttps://mcp-echo-server-<random>.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
AuthenticationNoAuthentication
Additional Propertysap-joule-studio-mcp-server = true

Important: The URL must NOT end with /mcp—Joule Studio adds that automatically! And be sure to set the additional properties so it is visible from Joule Studio.

 

Integrating with Joule Studio

With the server deployed and destination configured:

  1. Open Joule Studio
  2. Navigate to MCP Servers
  3. Click Add MCP Server
  4. Select your destination (mcp-echo-server)
  5. Configure:
    • Name: Echo Server
    • Path: /mcp (default)
  6. Save

Now test it! Ask Joule to use your echo tool by sending "foo"—you should get "bar" back.

 

One of the features that is really neat is the ability to see the execution log so you can see the steps that the agent is taking. This is a great way to help debug if you need to provide more context in your instructions. It is very important to provide the agent with the context it needs to truly be an agent and make decisions based on the tools or Joule skills it has been provided.

 

Key Learnings & Tips

Here are the most important things I learned during this project:

  1. Zod is required: The MCP SDK v1.26+ requires Zod schemas for tool input validation. Plain JSON Schema objects will fail at runtime.

  2. Transport matters: Joule Studio requires streamable HTTP transport. SSE-type destinations are not supported.

  3. URL formatting: Your destination URL must NOT end with /mcp. Joule Studio appends this automatically.

  4. Session management: The SDK handles sessions automatically via the mcp-session-id header—you don't need to implement this yourself.

  5. Structured logging: Use JSON-formatted logs for easier debugging in BTP. The Application Logging service will thank you.

 

What's Next?

This echo server is just the beginning. You can extend this pattern to:

  • Query SAP S/4HANA data and expose it as a Joule skill
  • Connect to HANA databases for real-time analytics
  • Trigger SAP Build Process Automation workflows from natural language
  • Integrate with SuccessFactors for HR-related agent capabilities

 

Troubleshooting

If you encounter issues:

  • Server won't start: Check the Cloud Foundry logs in BTP Cockpit
  • Joule can't connect: Verify the destination URL doesn't end with /mcp
  • Tool not showing in Joule: Confirm the sap-joule-studio-mcp-server property is set to true
  • Schema validation errors: Ensure you're using Zod schemas, not plain JSON Schema

 

Conclusion

Using AI (from SAP AI Core) to build a tool for AI (Joule Studio), deployed on AI-ready infrastructure (BTP Cloud Foundry)—it's BTP all the way down! The barrier to entry for building MCP servers is lower than ever, especially with AI-assisted development tools at your fingertips.

 

Have you built an MCP server for Joule Studio? Please comment below if you have any questions or want to share your experience.

 

Have a great day and happy agent building!

-Max McPhee

Resources

  • SAP Build Introduces New MCP Servers
  • Model Context Protocol Specification
  • Joule Studio Documentation
  • SAP BTP Cloud Foundry Documentation

entry.title: Part 2: Creating and Deploying a CAP Application with PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option
entry.content:
Note: This blog is the Second Part of series - Building and Deploying SAP CAP Applications on BTP: From Database to Work Zone.

 

Introduction

In the Part 1 of this series, a CAP application has been created and deployed to SAP BTP using SAP HANA Cloud. In the current blog, I will focus on creating and deploying a CAP application to BTP using the PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option provided by SAP. This approach demonstrates a PostgreSQL-first workflow and focuses on the specific configuration required to use PostgreSQL as the persistence layer.

 

Why PostgreSQL on SAP BTP

You might have a question, “If a CAP application can be deployed with SAP HANA Cloud, then why go with an alternative Database?”

While the SAP HANA Cloud is still the best choice for enterprise applications, CAP also allows seamless integration with alternative databases with minimal changes.

PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option on SAP BTP is useful in scenarios such as:

  • Cost-sensitive environments
  • Open-source aligned architectures
  • Hybrid cloud landscapes

From the CAP application perspective, the CDS model and service definitions remain unchanged.

 

Pre-requisites:

Please make sure that you have these before starting the development:

  • SAP BTP Account
  • Cloud Foundry instance
  • PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option service subcription
  • SAP BAS / VSCode for development
  • If using BAS, have a dev space running
  • If using VSCode then make sure that you have installed Node.js and CLI installed

Now, let’s start with the development.

 

Create a New CAP Project in SAP Business Application Studio

SAP Business Application Studio provides a template for creating a CAP project with the required structure. The positives of using the template is that it automatically builds your Multi-Tenant Application file (mta.yaml) with the required deployment modules.

 

Follow the following steps:

  • Open BAS
  • Click on the Side Menu on the top-left corner
  • Click on File
  • Click on New Project From Template
  • Select CAP Project
  • Configure the project as follows:
    • Project Name: cap-postgres-app
    • Runtime: Node.js
    • Database: PostgreSQL
    • Deployment Environment: Cloud Foundry
    • Runtime Capabilities: XSUAA
  • Click on Finish

CAP project creation wizard

 

Now that the project is created, let’s get into development.

 

Define the Data Model

If you have schema.cds under db/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have schema.cds, then create one.

namespace com.mycompany.capapp;

entity Products {
    key ID          : UUID;
        name        : String(100);
        description : String(500);
        price       : Decimal(10, 2);
        stock       : Integer;
        category    : String(50);
        createdAt   : Timestamp;
        modifiedAt  : Timestamp;
}

 

Expose the Model via a CAP Service

If you have service.cds under srv/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have service.cds, then create one.

using com.mycompany.capapp from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
    entity Products as projection on capapp.Products;
    }

CAP automatically exposes this service as an OData endpoint without requiring manual REST configuration.

 

Add Data

Run the following in BAS Terminal

cds add data –records 5

 

Run the Application locally

Run the following command:

cds watch

You should be able to see the data in the Products

You can also use the SQLite as a database while running locally. Execute these before starting the application:

cds build

cds deploy

 

Add a Managed Approuter

  1. Right click on mta.yaml
  2. Click on Create MTA Module from Template
  3. Select Approuter Configuration
  4. Fill in the required details
  5. Click Finish

This is important to view the data once deployed.

Prepare the Project for Deployment

Since we have created the project from the BAS Template, we already have the mta.yaml updated and deployment ready. This file already includes:

  • CAP Service Modules
  • SAP HANA HDI Container
  • Required Service Bindings

Though everything is ready, you need to make a small change for Postgres Deployment. Make the following change:

  • In MTA, go to resources
  • Navigate to <app-name>-postgres
  • Change the service-plan from development to trial

 

mta.yaml visible in the BAS workspace

 

Note : Do this only if you are using a Trial Account. If you are in an enterprise account, please go to your Instances and Subscription and copy the service-plan from PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option and look for the plan under Service Plan.

 

Configure CAP to Use PostgreSQL

Navigate into the project folder and install the PostgreSQL driver:

cds add postgres

Note: This step is not important as BAS Template must have already added this. This could be used while creating the CAP application without the Template wizard.

 

Build the CAP Project

First things first, install node modules. Run the following in the Terminal:

npm i

To build the project, you can either run the following in the terminal:

mbt build

or can do it manually:

  • Right click on mta.yaml
  • Select Build MTA Project

This will build your project for deployment. Watch-out at the project structure. You might see a new addition to the structure mta_archives which consists the mtar file of the project.

 

Deploy the Application

To deploy the application to your BTP using Cloud Foundry, you will have to login to Cloud Foundry. Run the following in Terminal:

cf login

This will ask for your subaccount credentials. Provide them and you will be logged in successfully. Remember to map the correct space and org.

Once you are logged in successfully, you are ready to deploy the application. Remember the file generated while building the CAP project, this is the same file that will be deployed.

Run the following command:

cf deploy mta_archives/cap-hana-app_1.0.0.mtar

or, you can just right click on cap-postgres-app_1.0.0.mtar under mta_archives folder, and click on Deploy MTA Archive.

Successful Cloud Foundry Deployment

 

During deployment

  • PostgreSQL credentials are injected automatically via service binding
  • CAP initializes the database schema
  • The application starts without additional configuration

Confirm the Deployed Instances

 

Verify the Deployed Application

When the deployment is successful, you will get a application route. Copy the same and open it in the new tab of your browser and access the service endpoint: /catalog/Products

If the deployment is successful, you can see the data displayed in a JSON format.

Browser showing the OData service response

 

Conclusion

Deploying an SAP CAP application with the PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option on SAP BTP highlights CAP’s database-agnostic architecture. The CDS models and service logic remain unchanged, while PostgreSQL is integrated seamlessly through minimal configuration and service binding.

This approach provides flexibility for cost-sensitive and open-source aligned environments while still leveraging SAP BTP’s enterprise capabilities such as automated schema deployment, secure credential management, and simplified lifecycle handling through Cloud Foundry and MTA deployment.

Overall, PostgreSQL on SAP BTP enables developers to build scalable, cloud-native CAP applications with greater persistence flexibility, while retaining the option to transition to SAP HANA Cloud in the future without significant code changes.

 

Next Steps

In the next part of this series, the deployed CAP application will be integrated with SAP Build Work Zone, demonstrating how backend services can be exposed through a secure, user-friendly interface to deliver a complete end-to-end enterprise application experience.
